questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Why is liquid water denser than steam?
options: The particles in steam are heavier than the particles in water.
The liquid particles stop moving completely.
Steam contains no energy so it spreads out.
The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
questions.explanation: Density depends on how much mass is packed into a given volume. Liquid particles are closer together than gas particles.
questions.senNote: Density depends on how much mass is packed into a given volume. Liquid particles are closer together than gas particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Why is liquid water denser than steam?
options: The liquid particles stop moving completely.
Steam contains no energy so it spreads out.
The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
The particles in steam are heavier than the particles in water.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
questions.explanation: Density depends on how much mass is packed into a given volume. Liquid particles are closer together than gas particles.

questions.question: Why is liquid water denser than steam?
options: Steam contains no energy so it spreads out.
The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
The particles in steam are heavier than the particles in water.

questions.correctAnswer: The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
questions.explanation: Density depends on how much mass is packed into a given volume. Liquid particles are closer together than gas particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Why is liquid water denser than steam? Choose the best answer.
options: The liquid particles stop moving completely.
Steam contains no energy so it spreads out.
The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
The particles in steam are heavier than the particles in water.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
questions.explanation: Density depends on how much mass is packed into a given volume. Liquid particles are closer together than gas particles.
questions.senNote: Density depends on how much mass is packed into a given volume. Liquid particles are closer together than gas particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A kettle gives off steam while liquid water remains inside it. Which explanation is best for why the liquid water is denser than the steam?
options: The particles are closer together in the liquid, so the same mass takes up less volume.
The particles in steam are lighter than the particles in liquid water.
The liquid particles stop moving, so they become heavier.
Steam contains no energy, so it rises and becomes less dense.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles are closer together in the liquid, so the same mass takes up less volume.
questions.explanation: Density depends on how much mass is packed into a volume. In the liquid, the particles are much closer together, so the same mass fits into a smaller space. A common wrong idea is that gas particles are lighter, but the particles themselves do not become lighter when a liquid turns into a gas.
questions.senNote: Liquid water is denser because its particles are closer together.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement is correct about liquid water and steam?
options: Steam is less dense because its particles are more spread out.
Steam is less dense because each particle becomes smaller.
Liquid water is less dense because its particles are heavier.
Liquid water is denser only because it has no internal energy.
questions.correctAnswer: Steam is less dense because its particles are more spread out.
questions.explanation: When water becomes steam, the particles spread out and occupy a much larger volume, so the density decreases. A common wrong idea is that the particles themselves become smaller, but it is the spacing between particles that changes.
questions.senNote: Gas particles are more spread out, not smaller.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two sealed containers each hold the same mass of water. In one container the water is liquid. In the other it is steam. Which conclusion can be drawn?
options: The steam must occupy a larger volume, so its density is lower.
The steam must have smaller particles, so its density is lower.
The liquid must have more mass packed into it, so its density is higher because it contains more particles.
Both have the same density because they contain the same mass.
questions.correctAnswer: The steam must occupy a larger volume, so its density is lower.
questions.explanation: Density depends on both mass and volume. If the mass is the same, the less dense substance must take up more volume. A common wrong idea is to focus only on the mass being the same, but the key step is linking lower density to larger volume.
questions.senNote: Same mass but lower density means larger volume.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Use particle ideas to explain density changes
questions.question: A student says, "Steam is less dense than liquid water because the particles shrink during boiling." Which response is best?
options: The student is wrong because the particles do not shrink; they become more spread out.
The student is right because particles lose mass when they boil.
The student is right because boiling removes internal energy from the particles.
The student is wrong because liquid particles stop moving and lock together.
questions.correctAnswer: The student is wrong because the particles do not shrink; they become more spread out.
questions.explanation: When liquid water becomes steam, the particles stay the same kind of particles but become much more spread out. That larger spacing increases the volume and lowers the density. A common wrong idea is that the particles themselves get smaller, but the change is in spacing, not particle size.
questions.senNote: Boiling changes particle spacing, not particle size.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which explanation best links the particle model to the lower density of steam compared with liquid water?
options: In steam, the particles are much further apart, so the same mass is spread through a larger volume.
In steam, the particles have disappeared, so there is less mass in the gas.
In steam, the particles stop colliding, so density no longer depends on volume.
In steam, the particles become lighter because energy is lost during the change of state.
questions.correctAnswer: In steam, the particles are much further apart, so the same mass is spread through a larger volume.
questions.explanation: The particle model explains density by how closely packed the particles are. Steam has the same kind of particles as the liquid, but they are much further apart, so the volume is larger and the density is lower. A common wrong idea is that the gas has less mass because particles disappear, but the key point is increased spacing.
questions.senNote: Same particles, larger volume, lower density.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A student compares liquid water with steam and says, "The steam is less dense because there is more empty space between the particles." Is this correct?
options: Yes, because the particles are more spread out in the gas.
Yes, because the particles in the gas are lighter.
No, because gas particles stop moving.
No, because density depends only on mass, not on volume.
questions.correctAnswer: Yes, because the particles are more spread out in the gas.
questions.explanation: The student is correct. In steam, the particles are much further apart, so the same mass occupies a larger volume and the density is lower. A common wrong idea is that density depends only on mass, but volume matters as well.
questions.senNote: More space between particles means lower density.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A balloon fills with steam from a kettle. Which statement is correct about the steam compared with the liquid water in the kettle?
options: The steam is less dense because its particles are more spread out.
The steam is less dense because its particles have less mass.
The steam is denser because gases rise.
The steam is denser because it has gained energy.
questions.correctAnswer: The steam is less dense because its particles are more spread out.
questions.explanation: Steam is less dense than liquid water because the particles are much more spread out, so the same mass occupies a larger volume. A common wrong idea is that the particles lose mass, but the particle spacing changes, not the particle mass.
questions.senNote: Steam is less dense because the particles are more spread out.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Choose the best explanation for density differences
questions.question: Which explanation best describes why a gas is usually less dense than the same substance as a liquid?
options: The particles are further apart in the gas.
The particles in the gas are smaller.
The particles in the gas stop moving between collisions.
The gas contains less energy than the liquid.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles are further apart in the gas.
questions.explanation: A gas is usually less dense because its particles are much further apart, so the same mass fills a larger volume. A common wrong idea is that gas particles are smaller, but the size of the particles is not the reason for the density change.
questions.senNote: Lower density in gases is mainly due to greater spacing.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two samples have the same mass. Sample A is liquid water and Sample B is steam. Which statement is correct?
options: Sample B must have a larger volume than Sample A.
Sample B must have smaller particles than Sample A.
Sample A must have no internal energy.
Sample A and Sample B must have the same density because the masses are equal.
questions.correctAnswer: Sample B must have a larger volume than Sample A.
questions.explanation: If the masses are equal, the less dense sample must take up more volume. Steam is less dense than liquid water because its particles are more spread out. A common wrong idea is that equal mass means equal density, but density depends on volume too.
questions.senNote: Same mass but lower density means bigger volume.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Identify a correct statement about density in different states
questions.question: Which statement is correct about liquid water and steam?
options: Liquid water is denser because its particles are closer together.
Steam is denser because gas particles are lighter and rise.
Liquid water is denser because its particles stop moving.
Steam is less dense only because energy disappears during boiling.
questions.correctAnswer: Liquid water is denser because its particles are closer together.
questions.explanation: Liquid water is denser because its particles are packed much closer together than in steam. That means more mass is packed into each unit volume. A common wrong idea is that particles stop moving in the liquid, but liquid particles still move randomly.
questions.senNote: Closer particle spacing gives the liquid a higher density.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Why is liquid water denser than steam? Choose the best answer.
options: Steam contains no energy so it spreads out.
The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
The particles in steam are heavier than the particles in water.
The liquid particles stop moving completely.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
questions.explanation: Density depends on how much mass is packed into a given volume. Liquid particles are closer together than gas particles.
questions.senNote: Density depends on how much mass is packed into a given volume. Liquid particles are closer together than gas particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Why is liquid water denser than steam? Choose the best answer.
options: The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
The particles in steam are heavier than the particles in water.
The liquid particles stop moving completely.
Steam contains no energy so it spreads out.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
questions.explanation: Density depends on how much mass is packed into a given volume. Liquid particles are closer together than gas particles.
questions.senNote: Density depends on how much mass is packed into a given volume. Liquid particles are closer together than gas particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block has a mass of 40 g and a volume of 6 cm^3. What is its density?
questions.explanation: Density equals mass divided by volume.
questions.senNote: Density equals mass divided by volume.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block has a mass of 50 g and a volume of 7 cm^3. What is its density?
questions.explanation: Density equals mass divided by volume.
questions.senNote: Density equals mass divided by volume.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block has a mass of 60 g and a volume of 8 cm^3. What is its density?
questions.explanation: Density equals mass divided by volume.
questions.senNote: Density equals mass divided by volume.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block has a mass of 70 g and a volume of 9 cm^3. What is its density?
questions.explanation: Density equals mass divided by volume.
questions.senNote: Density equals mass divided by volume.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block has a mass of 80 g and a volume of 10 cm^3. What is its density?
options: 9 g/cm^3
8 g/cm^3
questions.correctAnswer: 8 g/cm^3
questions.explanation: Density equals mass divided by volume.
questions.senNote: Density equals mass divided by volume.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block has a mass of 90 g and a volume of 5 cm^3. What is its density?
questions.explanation: Density equals mass divided by volume.
questions.senNote: Density equals mass divided by volume.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block has a mass of 100 g and a volume of 6 cm^3. What is its density?
questions.explanation: Density equals mass divided by volume.
questions.senNote: Density equals mass divided by volume.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A metal block has a mass of 54 g. When it is lowered into water, the water level rises from 20 cm^3 to 26 cm^3. What is the density of the block?
options: 9 g/cm^3
questions.correctAnswer: 9 g/cm^3
questions.explanation: First find the block's volume from the rise in water level: 26 - 20 = 6 cm^3. Then density = mass / volume = 54 / 6 = 9 g/cm^3. A common mistake is to divide by 26 cm^3, but that is the final reading, not the displaced volume.
questions.senNote: First find the block's volume from the rise in water level: 26 - 20 = 6 cm^3. Then density = mass / volume = 54 / 6 = 9 g/cm^3. A common mistake is to divide by 26 cm^3, but that is the final reading, not the displaced volume.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A cube has a mass of 100 g and a volume of 8 cm^3. Which statement is correct about the cube?
options: Its density is 12.5 g/cm^3.
Its density is 800 g/cm^3 because mass and volume should be multiplied.
Its density is 0.08 g/cm^3 because volume should be divided by mass.
It must be less dense than a larger object.
questions.correctAnswer: Its density is 12.5 g/cm^3.
questions.explanation: Density is mass divided by volume, so 100 / 8 = 12.5 g/cm^3. The size of another object does not tell you its density on its own. A common wrong idea is to multiply mass and volume, but density compares how much mass is packed into each unit of volume.
questions.senNote: Density is mass divided by volume, so 100 / 8 = 12.5 g/cm^3. The size of another object does not tell you its density on its own. A common wrong idea is to multiply mass and volume, but density compares how much mass is packed into each unit of volume.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Block A has a mass of 30 g and a volume of 9 cm^3. Block B has a mass of 40 g and a volume of 10 cm^3. Which statement is correct?
options: Block B is denser because it has the larger mass.
Block A is denser because 30 / 9 is smaller than 40 / 10.
Block B is denser because 40 / 10 = 4 g/cm^3, which is greater than 30 / 9 about 3.33 g/cm^3.
The two blocks have the same density because their masses are similar.
questions.correctAnswer: Block B is denser because 40 / 10 = 4 g/cm^3, which is greater than 30 / 9 about 3.33 g/cm^3.
questions.explanation: You must compare both densities, not just masses. Block A has density about 3.33 g/cm^3 and Block B has density 4 g/cm^3, so Block B is denser. A common mistake is to assume the larger mass always means greater density without checking volume as well.
questions.senNote: You must compare both densities, not just masses. Block A has density about 3.33 g/cm^3 and Block B has density 4 g/cm^3, so Block B is denser. A common mistake is to assume the larger mass always means greater density without checking volume as well.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A student says, "This larger block must be denser because it has a bigger volume." Which response is best?
options: Correct, because density depends only on volume.
Correct, because bigger objects always have more mass packed in.
Not necessarily, because density depends on both mass and volume.
Not necessarily, because large objects have no measurable density.
questions.correctAnswer: Not necessarily, because density depends on both mass and volume.
questions.explanation: Density tells you how much mass there is in each unit of volume, so you need both mass and volume to decide. A larger object can be less dense if its mass does not increase as much as its volume. A common wrong idea is that bigger automatically means denser.
questions.senNote: Density tells you how much mass there is in each unit of volume, so you need both mass and volume to decide. A larger object can be less dense if its mass does not increase as much as its volume. A common wrong idea is that bigger automatically means denser.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A rock has a mass of 50 g. The water level in a measuring cylinder rises from 30 cm^3 to 35 cm^3 when the rock is added. Which conclusion can be drawn?
options: Its density is 10 g/cm^3 because the displaced volume is 5 cm^3.
Its density is 1.43 g/cm^3 because the final reading is 35 cm^3.
Its density is 250 g/cm^3 because mass and volume should be multiplied.
Its density cannot be found because the rock is irregular.
questions.correctAnswer: Its density is 10 g/cm^3 because the displaced volume is 5 cm^3.
questions.explanation: For an irregular object, use displacement to find the volume. The rock's volume is 35 - 30 = 5 cm^3, so density = 50 / 5 = 10 g/cm^3. A common mistake is to divide by the final reading instead of the rise in water level.
questions.senNote: For an irregular object, use displacement to find the volume. The rock's volume is 35 - 30 = 5 cm^3, so density = 50 / 5 = 10 g/cm^3. A common mistake is to divide by the final reading instead of the rise in water level.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which method would best allow a student to find the density of an irregular stone?
options: Measure its mass, then find its volume from water displacement.
Measure only its volume because density does not depend on mass.
Measure its mass and multiply by its volume.
Heat the stone until its temperature changes.
questions.correctAnswer: Measure its mass, then find its volume from water displacement.
questions.explanation: To find density, you need both mass and volume. For an irregular stone, volume is found by how much water it displaces, then density = mass / volume. A common wrong idea is that volume alone is enough, but density always depends on both quantities.
questions.senNote: To find density, you need both mass and volume. For an irregular stone, volume is found by how much water it displaces, then density = mass / volume. A common wrong idea is that volume alone is enough, but density always depends on both quantities.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two blocks each have a mass of 60 g. Block X has a volume of 12 cm^3 and Block Y has a volume of 20 cm^3. Which block is denser?
options: Block X, because the same mass is packed into a smaller volume.
Block Y, because the larger block must be denser.
They have the same density because their masses are equal.
There is not enough information because density depends only on mass.
questions.correctAnswer: Block X, because the same mass is packed into a smaller volume.
questions.explanation: If two objects have the same mass, the one with the smaller volume is denser because more mass is packed into each cubic centimetre. A common wrong idea is that the larger object must be denser, but density depends on mass and volume together.
questions.senNote: If two objects have the same mass, the one with the smaller volume is denser because more mass is packed into each cubic centimetre. A common wrong idea is that the larger object must be denser, but density depends on mass and volume together.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A piece of metal has a mass of 40 g and displaces 8 cm^3 of water. What is its density?
options: 5 g/cm^3
4 g/cm^3
questions.correctAnswer: 5 g/cm^3
questions.explanation: The displaced water gives the metal's volume, so density = 40 / 8 = 5 g/cm^3. A common mistake is to multiply 40 by 8 or to invert the division, but density means mass per unit volume.
questions.senNote: The displaced water gives the metal's volume, so density = 40 / 8 = 5 g/cm^3. A common mistake is to multiply 40 by 8 or to invert the division, but density means mass per unit volume.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A sample has a mass of 50 g and a volume of 9 cm^3. What is its density?
questions.explanation: Density = mass / volume, so 50 / 9 = 5.56 g/cm^3 to 2 decimal places. A common mistake is to invert the calculation and do 9 / 50 instead.
questions.senNote: Density = mass / volume, so 50 / 9 = 5.56 g/cm^3 to 2 decimal places. A common mistake is to invert the calculation and do 9 / 50 instead.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A student records these measurements for a block: mass = 60 g, length = 5 cm, width = 2 cm, height = 1 cm. What is the density of the block?
options: 6 g/cm^3
questions.correctAnswer: 6 g/cm^3
questions.explanation: First find the volume of the block: 5 x 2 x 1 = 10 cm^3. Then density = 60 / 10 = 6 g/cm^3. A common mistake is to stop after finding the volume or to multiply mass by volume instead of dividing.
questions.senNote: First find the volume of the block: 5 x 2 x 1 = 10 cm^3. Then density = 60 / 10 = 6 g/cm^3. A common mistake is to stop after finding the volume or to multiply mass by volume instead of dividing.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block has a mass of 70 g and a volume of 5 cm^3. What is its density?
questions.explanation: Density equals mass divided by volume.
questions.senNote: Density equals mass divided by volume.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block has a mass of 80 g and a volume of 6 cm^3. What is its density?
questions.explanation: Density equals mass divided by volume.
questions.senNote: Density equals mass divided by volume.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement best describes internal energy?
options: It is only the energy stored in chemical bonds.
It is the same as gravitational potential energy only.
It is zero whenever a substance is not moving through space.
It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
questions.correctAnswer: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
questions.explanation: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.
questions.senNote: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement best describes internal energy?
options: It is the same as gravitational potential energy only.
It is zero whenever a substance is not moving through space.
It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
It is only the energy stored in chemical bonds.
questions.correctAnswer: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
questions.explanation: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.
questions.senNote: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.

options: It is zero whenever a substance is not moving through space.
It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
It is only the energy stored in chemical bonds.
It is the same as gravitational potential energy only.
questions.correctAnswer: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
questions.explanation: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.
questions.senNote: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.

options: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
It is only the energy stored in chemical bonds.
It is the same as gravitational potential energy only.
It is zero whenever a substance is not moving through space.
questions.correctAnswer: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
questions.explanation: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.
questions.senNote: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement best describes internal energy? Choose the best answer.
options: It is only the energy stored in chemical bonds.
It is the same as gravitational potential energy only.
It is zero whenever a substance is not moving through space.
It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
questions.correctAnswer: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
questions.explanation: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.
questions.senNote: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement best describes internal energy? Choose the best answer.
options: It is the same as gravitational potential energy only.
It is zero whenever a substance is not moving through space.
It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
It is only the energy stored in chemical bonds.
questions.correctAnswer: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
questions.explanation: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.
questions.senNote: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Use particle ideas to describe internal energy
questions.question: A hot metal spoon and a cool metal spoon are both at rest on a table. Which statement is correct?
options: The hot spoon has more internal energy because its particles have more kinetic energy.
Both spoons have zero internal energy because neither spoon is moving across the table.
The cool spoon has more internal energy because its particles are packed more closely.
Both spoons have the same internal energy because they are made of the same metal.
questions.correctAnswer: The hot spoon has more internal energy because its particles have more kinetic energy.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the energy stored in the particles, not the movement of the whole object. In the hotter spoon, the particles have more kinetic energy, so the internal energy is higher. A common wrong idea is that an object at rest has no internal energy, but its particles are still moving randomly.
questions.senNote: An object can be still overall but still have internal energy in its particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Choose the best explanation of internal energy
questions.question: Which explanation best describes internal energy in a substance?
options: It is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles.
It is only the movement of the whole object through space.
It is only the kinetic energy of the particles.
It is the same as the temperature of the substance.
questions.correctAnswer: It is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles.
questions.explanation: Internal energy includes both the kinetic energy of particle motion and the potential energy linked to the forces between particles. A common wrong idea is to treat internal energy as temperature only, but temperature is just related to average kinetic energy, not the full internal energy store.
questions.senNote: Internal energy includes both kinetic and potential energy of particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two identical blocks are heated. Block A rises in temperature. Block B melts at a constant temperature. Which statement is correct?
options: Both blocks gain internal energy, but Block B gains it without a temperature rise during melting.
Only Block A gains internal energy because Block B stays at the same temperature.
Only Block B gains internal energy because melting needs energy but heating does not.
Neither block gains internal energy because energy is used up during the change of state.
questions.correctAnswer: Both blocks gain internal energy, but Block B gains it without a temperature rise during melting.
questions.explanation: Heating increases internal energy. For Block A, the particles gain kinetic energy and the temperature rises. For Block B, energy is still transferred in during melting, but it changes the particle arrangement instead of raising temperature. A common wrong idea is that no temperature rise means no increase in internal energy.
questions.senNote: Internal energy can increase even when temperature stays constant during a change of state.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Reject a subtle misconception about internal energy
questions.question: A student says, "Internal energy is just the temperature of an object." Which response is best?
options: That is not correct because internal energy includes potential energy as well as the kinetic energy linked to temperature.
That is correct because temperature measures all the energy in a substance.
That is not correct because internal energy is only the movement of the whole object.
That is correct because particles stop moving when temperature is constant.
questions.correctAnswer: That is not correct because internal energy includes potential energy as well as the kinetic energy linked to temperature.
questions.explanation: Temperature is linked to the average kinetic energy of particles, but internal energy also includes potential energy due to forces between particles. A common wrong idea is to treat temperature and internal energy as exactly the same thing, but internal energy is broader.
questions.senNote: Temperature is part of the picture, not the whole of internal energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Apply internal energy to a real situation
questions.question: A mug of hot tea is left on a desk and cools down. Which statement is correct?
options: The internal energy of the tea decreases as energy is transferred to the surroundings.
The internal energy of the tea stays the same because the mug is not moving.
The internal energy of the tea increases because the tea is still above room temperature.
The tea loses all internal energy once its temperature starts to fall.
questions.correctAnswer: The internal energy of the tea decreases as energy is transferred to the surroundings.
questions.explanation: As the tea cools, it transfers energy to the surroundings, so its internal energy decreases. A common wrong idea is to think internal energy depends on the mug moving as a whole, but it depends on the energy stored in the particles.
questions.senNote: Cooling means energy leaves the tea, so its internal energy falls.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Compare internal energy in two situations
questions.question: Two identical metal balls are on a bench. One is warmer than the other. Which statement is most likely to be correct?
options: The warmer ball has greater internal energy because its particles have more kinetic energy on average.
The cooler ball has greater internal energy because it loses less energy to the surroundings.
Both balls have the same internal energy because they are the same size.
Both balls have zero internal energy because neither ball is moving across the bench.
questions.correctAnswer: The warmer ball has greater internal energy because its particles have more kinetic energy on average.
questions.explanation: For two identical objects, the warmer one usually has greater internal energy because its particles have more kinetic energy on average. A common wrong idea is that a stationary object has zero internal energy, but the particles inside are still moving randomly.
questions.senNote: Warmer identical objects usually have greater internal energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Identify a correct statement about internal energy
questions.question: Which statement is correct about internal energy?
options: It depends on the energy stored in the particles, not on whether the whole object is moving.
It is zero whenever an object is not moving through space.
It is only the potential energy between particles.
It disappears during changes of state.
questions.correctAnswer: It depends on the energy stored in the particles, not on whether the whole object is moving.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the energy stored in the particles of a system. It does not become zero just because the whole object is not moving from place to place. A common wrong idea is to confuse internal energy with the motion of the whole object.
questions.senNote: Internal energy is about particle energy, not whole-object motion.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Apply the idea of internal energy
questions.question: A pan of water is heated on a hob. Which explanation is best?
options: The internal energy of the water increases because energy is transferred to its particles.
The internal energy stays the same until the water starts boiling.
The internal energy increases only if the pan moves.
The internal energy decreases because heating makes particles spread out.
questions.correctAnswer: The internal energy of the water increases because energy is transferred to its particles.
questions.explanation: Heating transfers energy to the water, so its internal energy increases. The particles gain energy even before the water boils. A common wrong idea is that internal energy stays constant until a visible change happens, but it increases as soon as energy is transferred in.
questions.senNote: Heating a substance increases its internal energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Distinguish internal energy from movement of the whole object
questions.question: A book slides across a table and then comes to rest. Which statement is correct?
options: The book can still have internal energy when it is at rest.
The book has no internal energy once it stops moving.
Internal energy is the same as the book’s speed.
Internal energy only exists in gases.
questions.correctAnswer: The book can still have internal energy when it is at rest.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is stored in the particles inside the book, so it does not vanish when the book stops moving across the table. A common wrong idea is to confuse internal energy with the movement of the whole object.
questions.senNote: An object at rest can still have internal energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Use particle ideas to explain internal energy
questions.question: Which statement is correct about the internal energy of a solid?
options: It includes kinetic energy because the particles vibrate, and potential energy because forces act between them.
It is zero because the particles are fixed in place.
It is only kinetic energy because the particles are moving.
It is only potential energy because the particles cannot move from place to place.
questions.correctAnswer: It includes kinetic energy because the particles vibrate, and potential energy because forces act between them.
questions.explanation: Particles in a solid still vibrate, so they have kinetic energy, and there are also forces between them, so there is potential energy too. A common wrong idea is that particles fixed in position have no kinetic energy, but vibrating particles still have it.
questions.senNote: Particles in solids vibrate, so solids still have internal energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement best describes internal energy?
options: It is the same as gravitational potential energy only.
It is zero whenever a substance is not moving through space.
It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
It is only the energy stored in chemical bonds.
questions.correctAnswer: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
questions.explanation: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.
questions.senNote: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement best describes internal energy? Choose the best answer.
options: It is zero whenever a substance is not moving through space.
It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
It is only the energy stored in chemical bonds.
It is the same as gravitational potential energy only.
questions.correctAnswer: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
questions.explanation: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.
questions.senNote: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement best describes internal energy? Choose the best answer.
options: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
It is only the energy stored in chemical bonds.
It is the same as gravitational potential energy only.
It is zero whenever a substance is not moving through space.
questions.correctAnswer: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
questions.explanation: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.
questions.senNote: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A sample of ice has a mass of 0.3 kg. The specific latent heat of fusion is 334000 J/kg. How much energy is needed to melt it?
questions.explanation: Energy transferred for a change of state equals mass multiplied by specific latent heat.
questions.senNote: Energy transferred for a change of state equals mass multiplied by specific latent heat.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A sample of ice has a mass of 0.4 kg. The specific latent heat of fusion is 334000 J/kg. How much energy is needed to melt it?
questions.explanation: Energy transferred for a change of state equals mass multiplied by specific latent heat.
questions.senNote: Energy transferred for a change of state equals mass multiplied by specific latent heat.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A sample of ice has a mass of 0.5 kg. The specific latent heat of fusion is 334000 J/kg. How much energy is needed to melt it?
questions.explanation: Energy transferred for a change of state equals mass multiplied by specific latent heat.
questions.senNote: Energy transferred for a change of state equals mass multiplied by specific latent heat.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A sample of ice has a mass of 0.6 kg. The specific latent heat of fusion is 334000 J/kg. How much energy is needed to melt it?
questions.explanation: Energy transferred for a change of state equals mass multiplied by specific latent heat.
questions.senNote: Energy transferred for a change of state equals mass multiplied by specific latent heat.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A sample of ice has a mass of 0.7 kg. The specific latent heat of fusion is 334000 J/kg. How much energy is needed to melt it?
questions.explanation: Energy transferred for a change of state equals mass multiplied by specific latent heat.
questions.senNote: Energy transferred for a change of state equals mass multiplied by specific latent heat.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A sample of ice has a mass of 0.2 kg. The specific latent heat of fusion is 334000 J/kg. How much energy is needed to melt it?
questions.explanation: Energy transferred for a change of state equals mass multiplied by specific latent heat.
questions.senNote: Energy transferred for a change of state equals mass multiplied by specific latent heat.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 0.20 kg sample of ice at 0 C is just melted. The specific latent heat of fusion of ice is 334000 J/kg. How much energy was needed for the melting?
questions.explanation: Use E = mL for a change of state. Here, 0.20 x 334000 = 66800 J. A common mistake is to use 334000 J directly and forget that the sample has a mass of only 0.20 kg.
questions.senNote: Use E = mL for a change of state. Here, 0.20 x 334000 = 66800 J. A common mistake is to use 334000 J directly and forget that the sample has a mass of only 0.20 kg.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A student heats ice at 0 C until it has just melted. Which statement is correct?
options: Its temperature stays at 0 C while energy is used to separate the particles.
Its temperature rises because the particles stop moving.
Its mass decreases because some energy disappears.
Its particles become smaller as they melt.
questions.correctAnswer: Its temperature stays at 0 C while energy is used to separate the particles.
questions.explanation: During melting, energy is still transferred, but it is used to separate the particles rather than increase their average kinetic energy. That is why the temperature stays constant. A common wrong idea is that the particles stop moving, but they still move and vibrate.
questions.senNote: During melting, energy is still transferred, but it is used to separate the particles rather than increase their average kinetic energy. That is why the temperature stays constant. A common wrong idea is that the particles stop moving, but they still move and vibrate.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two 0.10 kg samples at 0 C are heated by identical heaters for the same time. Sample A is ice and Sample B is liquid water. After one minute, Sample A is still at 0 C but Sample B is warmer. Which explanation is best?
options: Sample A is using energy for melting, so its internal energy increases without a temperature rise.
Sample A receives less energy because ice cannot absorb heat.
Sample B has no internal energy, so its temperature rises more easily.
Sample A stays at 0 C because its particles stop moving.
questions.correctAnswer: Sample A is using energy for melting, so its internal energy increases without a temperature rise.
questions.explanation: When ice melts, the transferred energy increases internal energy by loosening the forces between particles, so the temperature can stay constant. The liquid water warms because its energy is increasing the particles' average kinetic energy. A common wrong idea is that constant temperature means no energy transfer.
questions.senNote: When ice melts, the transferred energy increases internal energy by loosening the forces between particles, so the temperature can stay constant. The liquid water warms because its energy is increasing the particles' average kinetic energy. A common wrong idea is that constant temperature means no energy transfer.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A heating curve for water has flat sections at 0 C and 100 C. Which statement is correct?
options: At both flat sections, energy is transferred without a temperature rise because the state is changing.
At 0 C only, no energy is transferred because the temperature is constant.
At 100 C, the particles stop moving so the temperature cannot rise.
At both flat sections, the particles become smaller.
questions.correctAnswer: At both flat sections, energy is transferred without a temperature rise because the state is changing.
questions.explanation: The flat sections show melting and boiling. In both cases, energy is still transferred, but it is used to separate particles and change state, so the temperature stays constant. A common wrong idea is that a flat line means the heater has stopped or no energy is being transferred.
questions.senNote: The flat sections show melting and boiling. In both cases, energy is still transferred, but it is used to separate particles and change state, so the temperature stays constant. A common wrong idea is that a flat line means the heater has stopped or no energy is being transferred.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement correctly compares specific heat capacity and specific latent heat?
options: Specific heat capacity is energy needed to change temperature; specific latent heat is energy needed to change state without changing temperature.
They both describe energy needed to raise temperature.
Specific latent heat only depends on the mass, not the substance.
Specific heat capacity is used when a substance melts but latent heat is used when it warms as a liquid.
questions.correctAnswer: Specific heat capacity is energy needed to change temperature; specific latent heat is energy needed to change state without changing temperature.
questions.explanation: Specific heat capacity describes changing temperature, while specific latent heat describes changing state at constant temperature. The key idea is whether the energy changes average kinetic energy or is used to separate particles. A common mistake is to treat the two ideas as the same because both involve heating.
questions.senNote: Specific heat capacity describes changing temperature, while specific latent heat describes changing state at constant temperature. The key idea is whether the energy changes average kinetic energy or is used to separate particles. A common mistake is to treat the two ideas as the same because both involve heating.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Steam condenses on a cold window. Which statement is correct?
options: The steam releases energy as it changes state, even though the particles themselves do not change.
The steam gains latent heat from the window.
The particles get heavier as they become liquid.
The temperature of the steam must rise while it condenses.
questions.correctAnswer: The steam releases energy as it changes state, even though the particles themselves do not change.
questions.explanation: Condensation is the reverse of boiling, so energy is released to the surroundings as the gas changes to a liquid. The particles themselves stay as water particles; only their spacing and arrangement change. A common wrong idea is that the particles change type or get heavier.
questions.senNote: Condensation is the reverse of boiling, so energy is released to the surroundings as the gas changes to a liquid. The particles themselves stay as water particles; only their spacing and arrangement change. A common wrong idea is that the particles change type or get heavier.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A heater transfers 334000 J to 1.0 kg of ice at 0 C. Which change would this energy produce?
options: It would just melt all the ice.
It would raise the ice to 334000 C.
It would boil the ice without melting it first.
It would halve the mass of the ice.
questions.correctAnswer: It would just melt all the ice.
questions.explanation: For ice, E = mL. With 1.0 kg and L = 334000 J/kg, 334000 J is exactly enough to melt the ice at 0 C. A common wrong idea is that all supplied energy must raise temperature, but during melting the temperature stays constant.
questions.senNote: For ice, E = mL. With 1.0 kg and L = 334000 J/kg, 334000 J is exactly enough to melt the ice at 0 C. A common wrong idea is that all supplied energy must raise temperature, but during melting the temperature stays constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A heating graph for ice rises to 0 C and then stays flat for a time. Which conclusion can be drawn from the flat section?
options: Energy is being used to melt the ice rather than raise its temperature.
The particles have stopped moving until the graph rises again.
No energy is being transferred because the temperature is constant.
The mass of the ice must be decreasing.
questions.correctAnswer: Energy is being used to melt the ice rather than raise its temperature.
questions.explanation: A flat section on a heating curve shows a change of state. The energy is still being transferred, but it is used to separate particles, so the temperature does not rise. A common wrong idea is that constant temperature means the heater is not doing anything.
questions.senNote: A flat section on a heating curve shows a change of state. The energy is still being transferred, but it is used to separate particles, so the temperature does not rise. A common wrong idea is that constant temperature means the heater is not doing anything.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 0.40 kg sample of ice at 0 C receives 66800 J of energy. The specific latent heat of fusion is 334000 J/kg. How much of the ice melts?
questions.explanation: Rearrange E = mL to m = E / L. So m = 66800 / 334000 = 0.20 kg. A common mistake is to assume all 0.40 kg melts, but the energy supplied is only enough for half the mass.
questions.senNote: Rearrange E = mL to m = E / L. So m = 66800 / 334000 = 0.20 kg. A common mistake is to assume all 0.40 kg melts, but the energy supplied is only enough for half the mass.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which process uses specific latent heat of vaporisation?
options: Changing liquid water at 100 C into steam at 100 C
Raising water from 20 C to 30 C
Cooling steam from 120 C to 100 C
Melting ice at 0 C
questions.correctAnswer: Changing liquid water at 100 C into steam at 100 C
questions.explanation: Specific latent heat of vaporisation is the energy needed to change a liquid into a gas without changing temperature. Melting uses latent heat of fusion instead. A common wrong idea is to confuse latent heat with simply heating a substance to a higher temperature.
questions.senNote: Specific latent heat of vaporisation is the energy needed to change a liquid into a gas without changing temperature. Melting uses latent heat of fusion instead. A common wrong idea is to confuse latent heat with simply heating a substance to a higher temperature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A sample of ice has a mass of 0.6 kg. The specific latent heat of fusion is 334000 J/kg. How much energy is needed to melt it?
questions.explanation: Energy transferred for a change of state equals mass multiplied by specific latent heat.
questions.senNote: Energy transferred for a change of state equals mass multiplied by specific latent heat.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A sample of ice has a mass of 0.7 kg. The specific latent heat of fusion is 334000 J/kg. How much energy is needed to melt it?
questions.explanation: Energy transferred for a change of state equals mass multiplied by specific latent heat.
questions.senNote: Energy transferred for a change of state equals mass multiplied by specific latent heat.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A sample of ice has a mass of 0.2 kg. The specific latent heat of fusion is 334000 J/kg. How much energy is needed to melt it?
questions.explanation: Energy transferred for a change of state equals mass multiplied by specific latent heat.
questions.senNote: Energy transferred for a change of state equals mass multiplied by specific latent heat.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement about particles in a solid is correct?
options: They are far apart and move freely in all directions.
They have no kinetic energy at all.
They fill the whole container because they can flow.
They vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.correctAnswer: They vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: In a solid, particles are close together and vibrate around fixed positions.
questions.senNote: In a solid, particles are close together and vibrate around fixed positions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement about particles in a solid is correct?
options: They have no kinetic energy at all.
They fill the whole container because they can flow.
They vibrate about fixed positions.
They are far apart and move freely in all directions.
questions.correctAnswer: They vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: In a solid, particles are close together and vibrate around fixed positions.
questions.senNote: In a solid, particles are close together and vibrate around fixed positions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement about particles in a solid is correct?
options: They fill the whole container because they can flow.
They vibrate about fixed positions.
They are far apart and move freely in all directions.
They have no kinetic energy at all.
questions.correctAnswer: They vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: In a solid, particles are close together and vibrate around fixed positions.
questions.senNote: In a solid, particles are close together and vibrate around fixed positions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement about particles in a solid is correct?
options: They vibrate about fixed positions.
They are far apart and move freely in all directions.
They have no kinetic energy at all.
They fill the whole container because they can flow.
questions.correctAnswer: They vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: In a solid, particles are close together and vibrate around fixed positions.
questions.senNote: In a solid, particles are close together and vibrate around fixed positions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement about particles in a solid is correct? Choose the best answer.
options: They are far apart and move freely in all directions.
They have no kinetic energy at all.
They fill the whole container because they can flow.
They vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.correctAnswer: They vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: In a solid, particles are close together and vibrate around fixed positions.
questions.senNote: In a solid, particles are close together and vibrate around fixed positions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement about particles in a solid is correct? Choose the best answer.
options: They have no kinetic energy at all.
They fill the whole container because they can flow.
They vibrate about fixed positions.
They are far apart and move freely in all directions.
questions.correctAnswer: They vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: In a solid, particles are close together and vibrate around fixed positions.
questions.senNote: In a solid, particles are close together and vibrate around fixed positions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Use particle ideas to describe a solid
questions.question: A metal tray keeps its shape when it is carried across a room. Which explanation is best?
options: Its particles are close together and only vibrate about fixed positions.
Its particles are far apart and flow freely through the tray.
Its particles stop moving completely, so the shape stays fixed.
Its particles become larger when the metal is solid.
questions.correctAnswer: Its particles are close together and only vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: A solid keeps its shape because its particles stay close together and vibrate about fixed positions instead of moving past each other. A common wrong idea is that particles in a solid stop moving, but they still vibrate.
questions.senNote: Particles in a solid vibrate but do not move past each other.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Choose the best explanation for particles in a solid
questions.question: Which statement is correct about particles in a solid?
options: They are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.
They are larger than gas particles of the same substance.
They are fixed because they have no kinetic energy.
They fill the container because they can flow.
questions.correctAnswer: They are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: Particles in a solid are packed closely and vibrate in fixed positions. A common wrong idea is that solid particles have no kinetic energy, but vibrating particles still have kinetic energy.
questions.senNote: Solid particles are close together and still vibrate.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Compare particle arrangements in different states
questions.question: A substance changes from a solid to a liquid. Which statement is correct?
options: The particles stay the same, but they are able to move past each other more easily.
The particles become smaller, so the substance can flow.
The particles stop vibrating and begin to disappear.
The particles become lighter, so the density must become zero.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles stay the same, but they are able to move past each other more easily.
questions.explanation: During melting, the particles do not change into different particles. They gain energy and are able to move past each other instead of only vibrating in fixed positions. A common wrong idea is that the particles themselves change size or type during a change of state.
questions.senNote: Changes of state change particle arrangement, not the particles themselves.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Reject a misconception about particles in solids and liquids
questions.question: A student says, "Particles in a liquid are fixed in place like particles in a solid." Which response is best?
options: The student is wrong because liquid particles can move past each other, even though they stay close together.
The student is right because liquids always keep a fixed shape.
The student is right because particles stop moving once a substance becomes liquid.
The student is wrong because liquid particles are much larger than solid particles.
questions.correctAnswer: The student is wrong because liquid particles can move past each other, even though they stay close together.
questions.explanation: Liquid particles are still close together, but they are not fixed in position. They can move past each other, which is why a liquid can flow. A common wrong idea is to assume that close particles must be fixed, but that is only true for a solid.
questions.senNote: Liquid particles are close together but not fixed in place.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Use particle ideas to explain a solid
questions.question: A cube of ice keeps its shape when placed on a plate. Which explanation is best?
options: Its particles only vibrate about fixed positions.
Its particles stop moving completely once they are cold.
Its particles are spread far apart so they cannot flow.
Its particles become heavier in the solid state.
questions.correctAnswer: Its particles only vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: Ice keeps its shape because its particles vibrate about fixed positions rather than moving freely past each other. A common wrong idea is that cold solid particles stop moving, but they still vibrate.
questions.senNote: A solid keeps its shape because the particles stay in fixed positions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Apply the particle model to a solid
questions.question: A student presses on a solid block and it does not flow to fill the container. Which statement is correct?
options: The particles are close together and cannot move past each other freely.
The particles have no energy at all.
The particles are farther apart than in a gas.
The particles change into different particles when pressed.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles are close together and cannot move past each other freely.
questions.explanation: A solid does not flow because its particles are held closely together and cannot move past each other freely. A common wrong idea is that the particles have no energy, but they still vibrate even in a solid.
questions.senNote: Solids do not flow because their particles cannot move past each other freely.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Compare solids and liquids using the particle model
questions.question: Which explanation best compares a solid with a liquid?
options: In both, particles are close together, but only in the liquid can they move past each other.
In both, particles are fixed in place and cannot move.
In the solid, particles are larger than in the liquid.
In the liquid, particles have no kinetic energy so they can flow.
questions.correctAnswer: In both, particles are close together, but only in the liquid can they move past each other.
questions.explanation: Both solids and liquids have particles that are close together. The difference is that liquid particles can move past one another, while solid particles only vibrate about fixed positions. A common wrong idea is that liquid particles are fixed in place like a solid.
questions.senNote: Solids and liquids both have close particles, but liquids can flow.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Choose a valid explanation using the particle model
questions.question: A solid iron bar is heated and still keeps its shape. Which statement is correct?
options: Its particles gain energy but still vibrate about fixed positions.
Its particles stop moving as it gets hotter.
Its particles become a different size when heated.
Its particles spread so far apart that the solid becomes a gas straight away.
questions.correctAnswer: Its particles gain energy but still vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: Heating a solid increases the energy of its particles, so they vibrate more, but they still remain in fixed positions unless the substance melts. A common wrong idea is that heating makes the particles stop moving or change size.
questions.senNote: Heating a solid makes its particles vibrate more strongly.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Use particle ideas in a real situation
questions.question: Mud can be poured, but a brick cannot. Which explanation is best?
options: The particles in the mud can move past each other, but the particles in the brick cannot.
The particles in the brick are larger than the particles in the mud.
The particles in the mud have no mass, so they can flow.
The particles in the brick stop moving completely.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles in the mud can move past each other, but the particles in the brick cannot.
questions.explanation: A material flows when its particles can move past each other. In a solid like a brick, the particles are held in fixed positions, so it keeps its shape. A common wrong idea is that flowing means particles are smaller, but the key difference is movement, not particle size.
questions.senNote: Flow depends on whether particles can move past each other.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Identify a correct statement about particles in a solid
questions.question: Which statement is correct about a solid object at room temperature?
options: Its particles vibrate, even though the object keeps a fixed shape.
Its particles are completely still.
Its particles are spread far apart and move randomly in all directions.
Its particles must be heavier than the particles in the same substance as a liquid.
questions.correctAnswer: Its particles vibrate, even though the object keeps a fixed shape.
questions.explanation: Particles in a solid still vibrate, even though the solid keeps its shape overall. A common wrong idea is that particles in solids are completely still, but they still have kinetic energy.
questions.senNote: Solid particles vibrate even when the solid looks still.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement about particles in a solid is correct? Choose the best answer.
options: They fill the whole container because they can flow.
They vibrate about fixed positions.
They are far apart and move freely in all directions.
They have no kinetic energy at all.
questions.correctAnswer: They vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: In a solid, particles are close together and vibrate around fixed positions.
questions.senNote: In a solid, particles are close together and vibrate around fixed positions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement about particles in a solid is correct? Choose the best answer.
options: They vibrate about fixed positions.
They are far apart and move freely in all directions.
They have no kinetic energy at all.
They fill the whole container because they can flow.
questions.correctAnswer: They vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: In a solid, particles are close together and vibrate around fixed positions.
questions.senNote: In a solid, particles are close together and vibrate around fixed positions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement about particles in a solid is correct? Choose the best answer.
options: They are far apart and move freely in all directions.
They have no kinetic energy at all.
They fill the whole container because they can flow.
They vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.correctAnswer: They vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: In a solid, particles are close together and vibrate around fixed positions.
questions.senNote: In a solid, particles are close together and vibrate around fixed positions.
